questions.question: Explain the Bellman-Ford algorithm for finding single-source shortest paths. Discuss its time complexity and how it handles negative weight edges. Also, explain what happens when a negative weight cycle exists in the graph.
keyPoints: Bellman-Ford relaxes all edges V-1 times to find shortest paths from source
Time complexity is O(VE) where V is vertices and E is edges
Can detect negative weight cycles by checking for further relaxation in V-th iteration
Works correctly with negative edges unlike Dijkstra's algorithm

questions.question: Compare Prim's and Kruskal's algorithms for finding Minimum Spanning Trees (MST). Under what practical circumstances would you prefer one algorithm over the other? Explain with an example.
keyPoints: Prim's grows single tree by adding nearest vertex (O(V²) with array, O(E log V) with heap)
Kruskalal sorts edges and adds minimum weight edge that doesn't form cycle using Union-Find
Prim's better for dense graphs, Kruskal's better for sparse graphs
Both guarantee MST but use different approaches: vertex-based vs edge-based

questions.question: (a) Describe the Floyd-Warshall algorithm for finding all-pairs shortest paths in a weighted graph. Provide its time and space complexity. (b) Compare and contrast Floyd-Warshall with Dijkstra's algorithm and Bellman-Ford, discussing their respective advantages, limitations, and suitable application scenarios. (c) Apply Floyd-Warshall to compute all-pairs shortest paths for a graph with 4 vertices and explain each iteration.
keyPoints: Floyd-Warshall uses dynamic programming, considers intermediate vertices, O(V³) time and O(V²) space
Dijkstra: O((V+E)logV) for single-source, no negative weights; Bellman-Ford: O(VE) but handles negatives
Floyd-Warshall ideal for dense graphs with small V, finds all-pairs paths; Dijkstra better for sparse graphs
Algorithm iterates through each vertex as potential intermediate, updates distance matrix

questions.question: (a) Trace through Kruskal's algorithm step-by-step to find the Minimum Spanning Tree for a given weighted graph with 6 vertices and 9 edges. Show how the Union-Find data structure prevents cycle formation. (b) Explain the cut property of MSTs and prove why Kruskal's algorithm produces a minimum spanning tree. (c) Discuss practical applications of MSTs in network design (telecommunications, road networks, cluster analysis) and explain why finding an optimal spanning tree is NP-hard in certain constrained scenarios.
keyPoints: Kruskal's sorts edges ascending, uses Union-Find with find and union operations to detect cycles
Cut property: for any cut in graph, minimum weight edge crossing cut belongs to some MST
Proof by cut property shows greedy choice is optimal; algorithm always produces MST
Applications: network routing, cable TV, airline routes, image segmentation, clustering
